It's not being about him. I'm like, you got to be shitting me. But there's a difference between not selling benefit and not selling outcome. So the typical I, we copy problem is the corporate brochure with dad founded the business in 1812, and we make the best steel girder, and we, we, we, we, we, we, we, we, we, we, right? And we know that's a problem. Generally speaking, if you narrow it now to selling something, the customer wants to hear about things phrased and framed as their experience, not as your thing, right? And there's copy devices for doing all of that, right? So we don't write about how we have at our restaurant this magnificent patio that replicates being in Monte Carlo overlooking the water. We simply, we start to substitute. Imagine being in Monte Carlo, so we substitute that kind of language, right? However, most copy, most sales pitches, however they're delivered in whatever media, print, direct mail, ad, ads are often space restricted. So there's an additional complication. Website, webinar, tell someone or whatever. Are not nearly enough about you, has they ought to be? Because as I said earlier, ultimately, that's the differentiator. That's it, right? One of the things I'm going to show you tonight, which was so far ahead of its time. Now, it's primitive by today's standards. And you'll be tempted to laugh at it, but you ought not. Because it's from the early 70s. So I'm going to show you a 50 minute video. And it's primitive by today's standards, but way ahead of its time for the 50s. And in this respect, it's still ahead of what most people do. So I'm going to show you a movie called, it's called Turner, Turner, Turner. And it was a recruiting film for the Glenn Turner companies when they were already in trouble. So it's a recruiting film for Coscott and Dare to Be Great that was shown at big opportunity meetings before they did the opportunity. And you will see it's really all about him. But in a way that delivers benefit and so forth, and you'll see that too. But if you compare it to almost any DVD presentation, any info marketer, opportunity seller, chiropractor, dentist, anybody is using today and sending out, forgetting the mechanics and the fact that they're wearing goofy clothes. But you'll see he did something nobody's doing in focusing it all on him. And you wanting to be so connected to him that the specifics didn't matter at all. You could plug anything into the back of it, and it wouldn't make any difference. The other thing I'll say quickly about just narrowing back to copy is a very big and common mistake, especially when you're selling to the same people over and over again, is not telling your whole story again and again and again and again and again. Because A, we think they already know it. And so they don't need to hear it again, which are really two entirely different things. They may know it, but that doesn't mean they don't need to hear it again. And we get sick and tired of telling it, so we don't feel like we want to tell it. But it's an integral part of the reason why. So it's a very common mistake. John could very easily make it. John Duquesne could very easily make it with himself or with his main asset, Pavel, the kettlebell king, because he sells the same customers over and over and over again. And he could stop telling the legend, thinking they already know the legend. They don't need to hear it again. And we're sick of it.
